name: Abstrakt
classification_notes: Optimo describes Abstrakt as drawing on early 20th century geometric sans-serif lettering built with compass and ruler, anchored in modernist ideas, with shapes that foreshadow the pop aesthetic of the early computer age.
summary: A geometric sans by François Rappo built from 1930s Swiss lettering, with a standard width and a Narrow companion across four weights and italics.
description: A geometric sans-serif by François Rappo that synthesises a 1930s Swiss lettering style into a full family. Each character is a slight abstraction of conventional letter shapes, drawn with compass-and-ruler logic that recalls Swiss architecture, graphic and industrial design from the period. Available in Light, Regular, Bold and Black with matching italics, plus a Narrow width with the same range, giving identity systems and editorial work a controlled set of geometric voices.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present Abstrakt as a François Rappo design rooted in early 20th century geometric sans-serif lettering, built from hand-picked artifacts of an avant-garde lettering style en vogue among graphic artists in the early 1930s. Reference points include the small characters on the 1932 Swiss exhibition lettering and the iconic BAG Turgi lamps, with shapes that already foreshadow the pop aesthetic of the early computer age.

name: Apax
classification_notes: Optimo positions Apax as Rappo's reading of constructivism through a modernist lens, combining a rigorous, systematic geometric architecture with the functional tradition of modern grotesque design.
summary: François Rappo's constructivist grotesque, geometric in architecture but optically tuned for fluent text and identity work.
description: A grotesque by François Rappo that explores the visual grammar of constructivism through a modernist lens. The geometric architecture produces radical shapes and many distinctive letters, while the optical balance Rappo is known for keeps the family fluent across body texts and visual identities. Six weights from Thin to Superbold come with italics, and each style includes alternate glyphs that let designers tune the constructivist character up or down.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Apax as François Rappo's exploration of constructivist visual grammar through a modernist lens. Released in 2016, it sits in the trilogy alongside Plain and Rand, presenting a rigorous, systematic geometric identity combined with the functionalist tradition of modern grotesque design.

name: Artex
classification_notes: Optimo describes Artex as bringing the neo-grotesque programme into the realm of variable fonts, with simplified forms that temper strict geometry and modular, symmetrical construction tuned for transitions across widths and weights.
summary: A neo-grotesque variable system by Valentin Brustaux, spanning compressed to extended widths and hairline to black weights, engineered for static and motion use.
description: A neo-grotesque by Valentin Brustaux that takes the genre into variable-font territory. The programme runs from compressed to extended through six widths and from hairline to black through six weights, delivered as 72 static fonts plus variable formats. Modular and symmetrical construction keeps the curves clean during weight and width transitions, and each style includes 29 alternate glyphs, so the family suits motion graphics, identity systems and editorial work that needs to flex from text to display.
foundry_blurb: Optimo position Artex as Valentin Brustaux's effort to bring the neo-grotesque programme into the realm of variable fonts. They highlight thoughtful, rational shapes engineered to optimise transitions between extreme widths and weights, modular and symmetrical forms within a modern architecture, and a 72-font static set plus variable formats with 29 alternate glyphs per style.

name: Basel
classification_notes: Optimo describes Basel as a dual programme rooted in modernist typography, with a Grotesk variant and a Classic variant that share a skeleton and weight gradation but differ in stroke contrast, plus a monospace cut.
summary: A two-variant Swiss modernist family by Chi-Long Trieu, pairing a low-contrast Grotesk with a higher-contrast Classic, plus matching monos and a connecting variable axis.
description: A modernist sans by Chi-Long Trieu built as a dual programme. Basel Grotesk reinterprets Swiss modernist letterforms with a fresh dynamism, while Basel Classic shares the same skeleton and weight gradation but introduces decisively contrasting strokes for editorial elegance. A variable axis bridges the two, and a Mono companion runs through both variants. Initiated in 2013 and grown through fashion and publishing usage, the family runs from Hairline to Super with italics and a Variable cut.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Basel as Chi-Long Trieu's innovative dual programme, composed of two variants, Grotesk and Classic, that share skeleton and weight gradation but differ in stroke contrast. They highlight Basel Variable as the bridge between the two and note the family was initiated in 2013, developed through publishing and fashion work, and offers worldly, refined characters for modern needs.

name: Cargo
classification_notes: Optimo describes Cargo as a headline typeface with curved stencil cuts carved within a geometric sans-serif framework, with rounded bridges that form connected open spaces between letters.
summary: A single-cut display stencil by Gavillet & Rust, drawn in 2002 for a Swiss music club identity and made for headlines and logos.
description: A geometric display stencil by Gavillet & Rust drawn for the visual identity of the 2002 Swiss National Exhibition's Cargo music club. The stencil cuts are curved rather than straight, and the rounded bridges form connected open spaces between letters that read as a single rhythm at large sizes. Loosely inspired by the logo of the stencil-machine company Marsh, the single-cut typeface stays at home in headlines, logos and identity work where its memorable silhouette can carry the design.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present Cargo as a Gavillet & Rust design intended for headlines, characterised by curved stencil cuts that create a graphic combination of letters. They cite the logo of stencil-machine company Marsh as a loose reference, and note the typeface was originally drawn for the 2002 Swiss National Exhibition's eponymous music club identity.

name: CEO
classification_notes: Optimo describes CEO as a monospaced typeface based on an almost rectangular module, capturing the modernist character of the 1960s Olympia mechanical typewriter and its Senatorial #71 typeface.
summary: François Rappo's monospace built on a near-rectangular module, drawn from the 1960s Olympia typewriter's Senatorial #71.
description: A monospaced typeface by François Rappo that captures the modernist fancy of the late mechanical typewriter era. Based on a nearly rectangular module, CEO retains the authoritative, systematic feel of its source while smoothing the curves into something more organic, with rounded stems and terminals and a careful eye on punctuation and special characters. Four weights from Light to Black with matching italics give editorial systems a typewriter voice that works in long blocks as well as quoted passages.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe CEO as François Rappo's monospaced design inspired by Senatorial #71, the typeface available on some Olympia typewriter models. They cite the Olympia line as developed under European General Electric (AEG), first produced in 1903 and discontinued in 1992, and characterise CEO as evoking the modular, visionary forms of the 1960s era.

name: Ceremony
classification_notes: Optimo describes Ceremony as a labels and icons typeface family that comes out of Studio Joost Grootens' information design practice, with an extended Latin character set sat alongside a large pictogram library.
summary: A 77-style labels and icons family by Studio Joost Grootens, built around 178 pictograms per style for atlases and information design.
description: A labels and icons family by Studio Joost Grootens that grew out of the studio's information design and atlas work. Across seventy-seven styles in Black, Outline, Sans and Sans Mono variants, each style carries an extended Latin character set and one hundred and seventy-eight pictograms, presented as positive and negative geometric shapes. Suited to wayfinding, data visualisation, atlases and any system that needs labelled symbols sitting in tight relation to type.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present Ceremony as Studio Joost Grootens' unprecedented labels and icons typeface family, derived from their information design practice. They cite Grootens' atlas work, including the Metropolitan World Atlas (2005), Atlas of the Conflict (2010) and Atlas of the Functional City (2015), and describe the family as seventy-seven styles each carrying one hundred and seventy-eight pictograms alongside an extended Latin character set.

name: Executive
classification_notes: Optimo describes Executive as a classic sans-serif typeface that retains the raw DNA of typewriter type, drawn from a Hammond typewriter shuttle with narrow proportions and generous letterspacing.
summary: Gavillet & Rust's typewriter-flavoured sans, drawn from a Hammond typewriter shuttle with narrow proportions and generous spacing.
description: A classic sans-serif by Gavillet & Rust drawn from a Hammond typewriter shuttle. The family keeps the source's narrow proportions, with W and M sitting compact, and the generous letterspacing reads clear and sharp at text sizes. Five weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics make it a workable text and identity sans with quiet typewriter character.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Executive as Gavillet & Rust's classic sans-serif drawn with the raw DNA of typewriter-generated type. They cite a Hammond typewriter shuttle as the trigger for the project, point to the narrow forms of W and M, and call out the generous letterspacing that gives the typeface its sharp, clear aspect.

name: Facility
classification_notes: Optimo describes Facility as flirting with the extravagance of the 1960s while staying grounded in Modernism, with distinctive curves and slightly flared terminals that evoke Verner Panton sat over a rational mid-century structure.
summary: Malte Bentzen's modernist sans with a 1960s flare and slightly flared terminals, in nine weights with italics and a variable axis.
description: A modernist sans by Zürich-based Danish designer Malte Bentzen that flirts with 1960s extravagance while staying grounded in mid-century rationality. Distinctive curves on letters such as a and s, paired with slightly flared terminals, evoke the vibrant shapes of Verner Panton, while the structural logic stays efficient and fluent. Nine weights with italics, plus an Extra Black, an Upright Variable and a full Variable, make it usable from text to display without losing the warmth that gives Facility its personality.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present Facility as a Malte Bentzen design that balances warmth with competence and projects confidence without austerity. They describe its distinctive curves and flared terminals as evoking Verner Panton, and frame it as the latest iteration of a long process of research, curiosity and refinement, with nine weights and italics.

name: Genath
classification_notes: Optimo describes Genath as a revival type based on a 1720 specimen including Johann Wilhelm Haas's first design for the Genath Foundry in Basel, slightly condensed, highly contrasting and Baroque in character.
summary: François Rappo's Baroque revival of Johann Wilhelm Haas's 1720 Genath Foundry types, with text and display optical cuts.
description: A Baroque revival serif by François Rappo, drawn from a 1720 specimen of Johann Wilhelm Haas's first design for the Genath Foundry in Basel. Slightly condensed and highly contrasting, the family keeps the playful, fancy details of the source while offering a clean digital interpretation. Text weights run from Light to Extrabold with italics, and a Display cut adds Thin and finer-tuned proportions for headlines and footnotes alike.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Genath as François Rappo's streamlined digitalisation of Baroque eclecticism and extravagance, based on Johann Wilhelm Haas's first design for the Genath Foundry in Basel. They place Haas (1698–1764) alongside Joan Michaël Fleischmann as marking a Northern European departure from Renaissance old styles, and present the family as truly Baroque, slightly condensed and highly contrasting.

name: Hermes
classification_notes: Optimo describes Hermes as a reinterpretation of the Epoca typeface produced by a Hermes 3000 typewriter, with simple geometrical shapes and low-contrast strokes adapted proportionally for legibility.
summary: A reinterpretation of the Hermes 3000 typewriter's Epoca face by Gavillet & Rust, extended to three widths and variable cuts in 2024.
description: A sans-serif by Gavillet & Rust, originally drawn in 2001 as a reinterpretation of the Epoca face produced by the Hermes 3000 typewriter. Soft curves, balanced geometry and low-contrast strokes carry a subtle retro charm without leaning kitsch. Redrawn and extended by Amélie Gallay in 2024, Hermes now runs as a collection across Narrow, standard and Wide widths in five weights with italics, plus variable fonts that bridge the widths and connect the gaps between the original Narrow and Wide families.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Hermes as Gavillet & Rust's reinterpretation of the Epoca typeface from the Hermes 3000 typewriter, the last typewriter Jack Kerouac owned. They cite the original 2001 release and the 2024 redrawing and extension by Amélie Gallay, which reformulated the typeface as a collection with variable fonts, additional styles and a new width bridging the original Narrow and Wide families.

name: JJannon
classification_notes: Optimo describes JJannon as Rappo's revival of seventeenth-century French type by Jean Jannon, preserving the asymmetrical axis, the small inclined bowl of the a, and the detailed cupped serifs of the original drawings.
summary: François Rappo's revival of Jean Jannon's seventeenth-century French type, with text and display cuts and Baroque swash details.
description: A Garalde revival by François Rappo that returns to the work of Jean Jannon, the Swiss-born printer whose Caractères de l'Université were long misattributed to Garamond. The drawing preserves the asymmetrical axis, the small inclined bowl of the a, the detailed cupped serifs and the sense of grandeur of the Baroque era. Text weights run from Book to Extrabold with italics and swash capitals, and a Display cut tightens the proportions for headline use.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present JJannon as François Rappo's meticulous study and revival of Jean Jannon's quintessential seventeenth-century French type. They describe Jannon's path from the Estienne atelier in Paris to Sedan and the Calvinist Academy, his 1641 commission from the Imprimerie Royale, and the project Caractères de l'Université that came out of it. The family preserves the asymmetrical axis, inclined bowl of the a, and cupped serifs of the original drawings.

name: Material
classification_notes: Optimo describes Material as a contemporary slab-serif inspired by Breite fette Egyptienne from the Bauer Foundry, itself a revival of Vincent Figgins's Antique, redrawn into a versatile multipurpose family with Niklas Herrmann.
summary: A contemporary slab-serif by Nicolas Eigenheer and Niklas Herrmann, redrawn from a Bauer Foundry Egyptienne for text and display.
description: A slab-serif by Nicolas Eigenheer, originally drawn from Breite fette Egyptienne, a Bauer Foundry revival of Vincent Figgins's Antique that traces back to early 19th century attention-commanding posters. Two decades after the first drawing, Eigenheer and Niklas Herrmann redrew the family, stripping it back to its essential forms and adding nuanced intermediate styles so it works at small sizes as well as large display settings. Five weights from Thin to Black with italics and an Upright Variable and full Variable.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Material as a contemporary slab-serif designed by Nicolas Eigenheer and entirely redrawn two decades later with Niklas Herrmann. They trace the inspiration to Breite fette Egyptienne from the Bauer Foundry, itself a revival of Vincent Figgins's Antique, and present the redrawn family as a multifunctional system with a soft, rhythmic texture and a distinctive block-like horizontal serif.

name: Media77
designer: Team'77 (André Gürtler, Christian Mengelt, Erich Gschwind)
classification_notes: Optimo describes Media77 as a digital revival of Media, a text typeface created by Team'77 in 1974 specifically for phototypesetting technology, redrawn by original members Christian Mengelt and Erich Gschwind.
summary: A revival of Team'77's 1974 phototypesetting text serif, redrawn by Christian Mengelt and Erich Gschwind for contemporary use.
description: A text serif by Team'77, originally drawn in 1974 for Bobst Graphic in Lausanne, specifically for phototypesetting technology. Where competing designers fought the technical limits of photo composition, Team'77 used them as a starting point for a typeface free of historical classification. Decades later, Christian Mengelt and Erich Gschwind redrew Media for digital use, keeping its sophistication and easy legibility at small sizes while preserving the refined details that read at display. Four weights with italics and a Condensed cut.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present Media77 as a newly digitalised version of Media, originally created in 1974 by Team'77 (André Gürtler, Christian Mengelt and Erich Gschwind) on commission from Bobst Graphic in Lausanne for phototypesetting. They highlight Team'77's Swiss Grand Award for Design (2015) and describe the redrawn family as extraordinarily sophisticated, very legible at small sizes and full of refined details at display sizes.

name: Metodo
classification_notes: Optimo describes Metodo as Davide Tomatis's evolution of a German type lineage that traveled European foundries under names including Neue Moderne Grotesk (Wagner & Schmidt), Aurora (Weber), Accidenz Grotesk (Haas) and Cairoli (Nebiolo).
summary: Davide Tomatis's revival of an early-20th-century European grotesque, with text and Mono cuts that carry distinctly metal-type curves into digital.
description: A grotesque by Italian designer Davide Tomatis, drawn from his master's project at ECAL in 2019. Metodo evolves a lineage of typefaces inspired by Neue Moderne Grotesk, the Wagner & Schmidt design that traveled European foundries as Aurora, Accidenz Grotesk, Cairoli and other names. Tomatis preserves the rounded shapes and tensions of the original metal type while drawing for digital systems. Five weights with italics, plus a Mono variant in matched weights with italics for code and editorial use.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Metodo as Davide Tomatis's substantive evolution of a lineage of European grotesques inspired by Neue Moderne Grotesk, originally developed by Wagner & Schmidt. They trace the typeface's pseudonyms across Weber (Aurora), Haas (Accidenz Grotesk) and Nebiolo (Cairoli) catalogues, and frame Metodo as a quantum leap from metal to digital while preserving the original's curves and tensions.

name: NEXT
classification_notes: Optimo describes NEXT as designed at the intersection of constructivism and humanism, with a binary programme of Book and Poster variants, originally drawn for the Museum of Modern Art in Warsaw identity.
summary: Ludovic Balland's binary Book and Poster system, originally drawn for the Museum of Modern Art in Warsaw, with Mono and Pan companions.
description: A sans-serif by Ludovic Balland that sits at the intersection of constructivism and humanism. Initiated in 2007 for the Museum of Modern Art in Warsaw and partly inspired by Marek Sigmund's design for the Polish Ministry of Transportation, NEXT is built as a binary programme: each weight comes as Book, with linear flow for text, and Poster, where narrow and wider letters create dynamic visual rhythm. A Mono variant and Pan Book and Pan Poster cuts round out the family, giving art and culture identities a coherent typographic toolkit.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present NEXT as Ludovic Balland's design at the intersection of constructivism and humanism. They cite the 2007 commission for the Museum of Modern Art in Warsaw and the partial inspiration from Marek Sigmund's design for the Polish Ministry of Transportation. NEXT is described as having a binary programme: Book versions provide linear flows, Poster versions offer dynamic visual rhythms from narrow and wider letters.

name: Pharma
classification_notes: Optimo describes Pharma as a sans-serif inspired by the interconnection between organic and technological aesthetics, with rounded forms at junctions and terminals that act as either soft joints or ink traps.
summary: Julien Gaillardot's organic-technological sans, with rounded junctions and ink-trapped terminals adapted for print and screen.
description: A sans-serif by Julien Gaillardot, initiated in 2000, that explores the interconnection between organic and technological aesthetics. Condensed geometric letterforms mutate along multiple axes, with rounded forms appearing at junctions and terminals as soft joints or, in other instances, as ink traps. Two weights with italics in standard and Condensed widths give the family a pragmatic range tuned for both print and screen.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Pharma as Julien Gaillardot's sans-serif inspired by the interconnection between organic and technological aesthetics. They note the project began in 2000 with condensed geometric letterforms that mutate along multiple axes, with rounded junctions and terminals that bring an organic quality to an angular structure, and that the typeface has been adapted for both print and screen.

name: Plain
classification_notes: Optimo describes Plain as Rappo's investigation of the rational simplicity of modernism, neither constrained by a strictly geometric approach nor by 19th-century grotesque idiosyncrasies, with a footnote-to-poster size range.
summary: François Rappo's modernist grotesque in a very wide weight range, with Arabic and Pan companions for international systems.
description: A modernist grotesque by François Rappo that follows the lineage of his Theinhardt revival. Plain is incredibly fluid; text composes effortlessly balanced lines and works from footnote to poster sizes. The drawing is neither strictly geometric nor tied to 19th-century grotesque quirks, which leaves the typeface universal but recognisable. The family stretches across an unusually large weight range with italics, plus a Skeleton cut, an Arabic companion in matched weights and a Pan version for cross-script identity work.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Plain as François Rappo's investigation of the rational simplicity of modernism, brought to a new level of achievement. They cite Theinhardt as the precursor and present Plain as the result of years of research, offering an exceptionally large range of weights, plus an Arabic companion, designed to function as a multi-sided tool for graphic designers.

name: Practice
classification_notes: Optimo describes Practice as a serif typeface whose architecture revisits Renaissance typography while introducing an innovative calligraphic drawing with carefully orchestrated sharp serifs.
summary: François Rappo's sharp Renaissance-inspired serif with text and display cuts, plus Pan companions for cross-script work.
description: A serif by François Rappo whose architecture revisits the elegance of Renaissance typography while pushing it into a crisp digital register. Practice is characterised by elegant proportions and sharp, precisely cut serifs, and the optical balance of the text rhythm reads cleanly on paper and on screen. Text and Display cuts give the family the range to move between long-form editorial and headline work, and Pan variants handle cross-script identity systems.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Practice as a significant outcome of François Rappo's evolving design practice, anchored in the research of typographic paradigms. They frame it as introducing a new perspective on serif typography with unprecedented sharpness, an innovative calligraphic drawing, and an architecture that revisits Renaissance elegance for crisp digital identity.

name: Programme
classification_notes: Optimo describes Programme as a typeface based on both calligraphy and computer programming, developed with scripting technology, where the full Latin alphabet is generated from the calligraphic letters o and n.
summary: A calligraphic-programmatic sans by Maximage, generated by script from the letters o and n, with text, Pan and Primitiv variants.
description: A sans-serif by Maximage developed with scripting technology, based on the idea that a typeface's DNA is contained in the calligraphic letters o and n. From those two glyphs, a computer programme builds the full Latin alphabet, since type ultimately reuses a limited set of shapes. Optimised for both text and display, Programme runs across five weights with italics, plus Pan companions and a Primitiv cut. First version featured in JRP Ringier's 2009 book Typeface as Program.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present Programme as Maximage's innovative typeface based on both calligraphy and computer programming. They describe the process of generating the full Latin alphabet from the calligraphic letters o and n via a script, and note that the first version was featured in JRP Ringier's 2009 book Typeface as Program, drawn from typeface and programming projects at ECAL.

name: Rand
classification_notes: Optimo positions Rand as the conclusion of Rappo's grotesque trilogy after Plain (2014) and Apax (2016), exploring the warmth that brought spirit to the rigidity of modern design, notably in The New York School.
summary: Closing chapter of Rappo's grotesque trilogy after Plain and Apax, drawn for warmth in the rigidity of modern design, with a Mono companion.
description: A grotesque by François Rappo that closes the trilogy of Plain (2014) and Apax (2016). Where Plain investigates rational modernist simplicity and Apax re-evaluates constructivist visual grammar, Rand explores the warmth and spirit that emerged from The New York School. Seven weights with italics give the family a balanced editorial range, and Rand Mono, with its shortened capitals and considered proportions, completes the system for code and tabular work.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Rand as the conclusion of François Rappo's trilogy on the grotesque genre, after Plain (2014) and Apax (2016). They frame it as exploring the shapes that brought spirit and warmth to the rigidity of modern design, notably emerging from The New York School, and point to Rand Mono as the companion that completes the system.

name: Recital
classification_notes: Optimo describes Recital as straddling Old-Style and Transitional, with substantial serifs, robust stems, slender lowercase proportions and more generous spacing in uppercase, in five weights with italics and variable axes.
summary: Malte Bentzen's contemporary serif sat between Old-Style and Transitional, in five weights with italics, plus Upright and Italic variable axes.
description: A contemporary serif by Malte Bentzen that defies neat classification, sat between Old-Style and Transitional. Substantial serifs and robust stems give the typeface a strong, resolute presence, while slender lowercase proportions and more generous uppercase spacing keep the rhythm warm. Five weights with matching italics, plus Upright Variable and Italic Variable axes and a full Variable, suit editorial systems that need to move between print and screen without losing character.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present Recital as Malte Bentzen's carefully orchestrated achievement, an exceptional fusion of references, emotions and dedication. They describe it as straddling Old-Style and Transitional, drawing on revivals nested within revivals, with substantial serifs, robust stems and a five-weight family that adapts seamlessly between print and screen.

name: Reply
classification_notes: Optimo describes Reply as a geometric typeface loosely inspired by Frank Lloyd Wright's correspondences, which used a typewriter version of Vogue Intertype, an American Art Deco-Constructivist sans of the early 1930s.
summary: François Rappo's geometric mid-century sans inspired by Frank Lloyd Wright's correspondence typewriter version of Vogue Intertype.
description: A geometric sans by François Rappo, drawn from research into American ephemera of the 1950s. Where Frank Lloyd Wright's preferred correspondence typeface was a typewriter version of Vogue Intertype, an American Art Deco-Constructivist sans cut in the early 1930s, Rappo took the simplified, refined drawing and reimagined it for contemporary use. Six weights with italics give Reply a colourful personality fit for editorial systems and identity work that wants a quiet mid-century anchor.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Reply as François Rappo's geometric typeface loosely inspired by Frank Lloyd Wright's correspondences, which used a typewriter version of Vogue Intertype. They place Vogue Intertype as a 1930s American sans-serif born from the migration of European designers who brought Art Deco and Constructivism across the Atlantic, and frame Reply as a contemporary read on a pivotal mid-century moment.

name: Ritmica
classification_notes: Optimo describes Ritmica as aligned with mid-century grotesque traditions through its horizontal terminals and straightforward stance, distinguished by almost-sealed negative spaces in the a, e and s.
summary: François Rappo's warm modernist grotesque, with almost-sealed counters that give the family a hypnotic rhythm, in five weights with italics and variable cuts.
description: A warm modernist grotesque by François Rappo. Aligned with mid-century grotesque traditions through its horizontal terminals and straightforward stance, Ritmica reveals personality through almost-sealed negative spaces in the a, e and s, which clasp letterform and counterform together and create a hypnotic, almost magnetic texture. Five weights with italics, plus an Upright Variable, Variable and Collection Upright and Italic Variable, give the family a tight editorial range with depth.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Ritmica as a warm modernist typeface from François Rappo that balances legibility with personality. They highlight the almost-sealed negative spaces in the a, e and s as the defining detail, frame it as drawing from early modernist candor, and present it as a demonstration of Rappo's mastery of pacing and proximity.

name: Stanley
classification_notes: Optimo describes Stanley as inspired by Times New Roman, sharper and more angular, with wide honed counter-forms, short ascenders and descenders, and a Poster style with greater contrast.
summary: Ludovic Balland's sharp, angular Times-inspired serif, with text weights and a higher-contrast Poster cut.
description: A serif by Ludovic Balland inspired by Times New Roman. Stanley keeps the legibility and economy of its reference but sharpens the shapes into something more angular and contemporary. Wide honed counter-forms and short ascenders and descenders make it efficient at small sizes, while the Poster style increases contrast and graphic clarity for headlines. Regular and Bold weights with italics, plus a Poster cut, named for Stanley Morison, the typographer behind Times New Roman.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Stanley as Ludovic Balland's typeface inspired by Times New Roman, sharper and more angular while preserving the essential qualities of its reference. They cite Stanley Morison's 1932 brief for The Times, which led to Times New Roman, and present Stanley as offering excellent legibility and sharpness at small sizes, with a Poster style that increases contrast and graphic clarity.

name: SuperScotch
classification_notes: Optimo describes SuperScotch as a Scotch-face sat halfway between modern and transitional, drawn without interpolation across each weight, inspired by mountaineering literature and Victorian-era Alpine typography.
summary: François Rappo's Scotch-face from the alignment of mountaineering literature and type design, with text and display cuts drawn without interpolation.
description: A Scotch-face serif by François Rappo, drawn from his interest in both mountaineering and type design. Starting from The Playground of Europe, an early classic of mountaineering literature that brought together the Alps, British explorers and a Scotch-face, Rappo set out to remake the genre with new curves and structural tensions. Sat halfway between modern and transitional, the family carries a Victorian sensibility into a versatile contemporary system, with each weight drawn individually for true optical balance. Text and Display cuts run from Thin through Extra with italics.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present SuperScotch as François Rappo's typeface emerging from the alignment of his interests in mountaineering and type design. They cite The Playground of Europe as the point of departure, frame the typeface as positioned halfway between modern and transitional faces with a Victorian-era sensibility, and note that each weight was drawn individually without interpolation.

name: Theinhardt
classification_notes: Optimo describes Theinhardt as François Rappo's revival of late-19th and early-20th-century sans-serif typefaces, named after Ferdinand Theinhardt, with nine weights and italics plus Compact, Condensed, SemiCondensed, SemiExtended, Extended, Mono and Pan companions.
summary: François Rappo's grotesque revival named for Ferdinand Theinhardt, in nine weights with italics across six widths, plus Mono and Pan.
description: A milestone grotesque revival by François Rappo, drawn from his study of late-19th and early-20th-century sans-serif type. Named after Ferdinand Theinhardt, whose work shaped the genre, the family is composed of nine complementary weights with italics that hold their character at every step. The system extends across Compact, Condensed, SemiCondensed, standard, SemiExtended and Extended widths, plus a Mono companion and a Pan version for cross-script systems. Considered the original grotesque par excellence, Theinhardt is engineered for extensive modern usage.
foundry_blurb: Optimo describe Theinhardt as a milestone in the development of grotesque type design, designed by François Rappo after studying the origins of sans-serif typefaces from the late 19th and early 20th centuries. They credit Ferdinand Theinhardt's visionary approach with shaping modern typography, and frame the family as nine complementary weights with matching italics combining the best historical features of early grotesques in a contemporary adaptation.

name: Void
classification_notes: Optimo describes Void as an original display typeface drawn from a research project into abstract typographic shapes not defined by standard writing tools, with blobby forms reminiscent of solvent molecules.
summary: Malte Bentzen's blobby display typeface from a research project into abstract typographic shapes, in three weights with a variable axis.
description: A display typeface by Malte Bentzen drawn from a research project into abstract typographic shapes that are not defined by standard writing tools. Suspended between past and future, Void's blobby shapes carry a magnetic fluidity reminiscent of solvent molecules, challenging the rational basis of the alphabet. After interest from designers wanting to use the lettering, Bentzen developed the project into a complete typeface with Thin, Regular and Bold weights and an Upright Variable axis.
foundry_blurb: Optimo present Void as Malte Bentzen's outcome of a research project into abstract typographic shapes that are not defined by standard writing tools. They quote Lucretius ("There can be no center in infinity"), describe the blobby shapes as suspended between past and future, and note that the project became a complete typeface after interest from designers who wanted to use the lettering.
